content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Whats the best MURDER mystery thriller you have ever watched ???

**Score:** 25
**Body:** What’s the best murder mystery thriller you’ve ever watched? I’m looking for something that genuinely keeps you guessing till the very end, with clever twists, strong atmosphere, and a payoff that actually feels earned. 

[](https://www.reddit.com/submit/?source_id=t3_1rwti91&composer_entry=crosspost_nudge)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This will make me seem so old! But , I have to say the best in this category for me would have to be Agatha Christie's ' Death on the Nile ' ( original) 
Brilliant mystery thriller, and the ultimate Whodunnit 
2. The original swedish Girl with the Dragon Tattoo. 
3. Last of Sheila
4. Prisoners (kidnapping), silence of the lambs, Wind River, Seven - so many! 
5. No Way Out still blows me away 
6. Forgotten

Memories of murder 

Prisoners 
7. The usual suspects and Identity
8. Mystic River (Sean penn) 

Its so so sooooo good
9. Clue! You didn’t specify it couldn’t be funny as well. 
10. The invisible guest (2016) 

## 2. Movies about growing old, dying, death of loved ones , fear of death

**Score:** 24
**Body:** As you can see I'm having a thing with death and being afraid of it. Don't know how it built up in me all of a sudden but here we are 



I want some media to interact with it in a positive or negative way

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Big Fish (2003)" - it has themes that you mentioned, but it has a positive spin on it, and it builds hope in whenever time does not seem to be on our side. Also, I hope that all becomes well for you very soon. Good day!
2. On Golden Pond
3. Train Dreams
4. Harold and Maude 
5. What Dreams May Come
6. Syndedoche, New York. Found that movie suggestion on this sub and I thought it was great.
7. His Three Daughters (2023)

Tuesday (2023)



8. Bicentennial Man
9. Plan 75 (2022), directed by Chie Hayakawa

After Life (1998), directed by Hirokazu Kore-eda
10. On Golden Pond

## 3. Does anyone know of any more horror movies about wild animals?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I watched a horror movie called Anaconda, and I'd really like to watch other movies similar to it, but since I'm very inexperienced in the world of movies, I'm asking this forum for help. I would be very grateful if someone could help me.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jaws is absolutely your next film to watch, the gold standard of these. Then pick from the rest below!

Lake Placid

The Grey

Crawl

The Ghost And The Darkness

The Edge

The Shallows

Primeval

And then Deep Blue Sea, Piranha 3D, and Snakes On A Plane are campy but fun to watch.
  - Literally OP watch Jaws immediately and come back and tell us how it was
  - ghost and the darkness (being based on a true story) is awesome
2. There were a lot of these movies in the 90s, and the reason is because they were all knocking off *Jurassic Park*, so if you haven’t seen Jurassic Park, that’s a run-don’t-walk type situation.

For one that’s a very similar kind of goofy as Anaconda, check out Deep Blue Sea! One of my favorites.
3. Cocaine Bear
4. Arachnophobia (horror/comedy)

Brotherhood of the Wolf (horror/martial arts/political/historical)
5. Lake Placid
6. The Bay is about flesh-eating lice. 
7. Does Tusk count?
8. Roar (1981) is gonna blow your mind. People were injured for real during the production.

## 4. What's the most disturbing/messed up movie you've ever watched? Looking for recommendations.

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Looking for recommendations, movies that genuinely stuck with you, made you uncomfortable, or u couldn't stop thinking about after watching. 

Any genre is fine, doesn't have to be horror 🫣

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Happiness 


2. Its been years since I've seen "the house that jack built" but I still think about it regularly. 
3. Antichrist
4. Threads. Two scenes in particular, the mom with her baby and the final scene. The rest is dreary AF too. 
5. Speak No Evil (2022), a Danish-Dutch psychological horror movie. It's messed up.

It has a 2024 English remake starring James Mcavoy (who's amazing), don't wanna spoil it for people who haven't watched but English remake has a different ending.
6. Mysterious Skin (2004) Was a rough watch 
7. The Coffee Table, Hereditary
8. Most, I'm unsure but I just rewatched The Killing of a Sacred Deer. 
9. [Funny Games (2008)](https://reelgood.com/movie/funny-games-2007) - Home invasion horror film where the antagonist knows he's in a horror film and makes it clear that all the violence and torture is for you.  The [original film (1997)](https://reelgood.com/movie/funny-games-1997) is shot for shot the same and by the same director, just with in German.

Nightmare Movies on youtube has a ton of recs like that.  Most of which, I am unwilling to watch.
10. Requiem for a Dream

## 5. Thriller movie recommendations

**Score:** 12
**Body:** I like to watch psycological thrillers mostly but i'm wanting to explore more of thriller category not just psycological thrillers that keep me at the edge of my seat. Something indulging so much so that it feels useful to watch it. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wind River 
2. Collateral
3. Get Out
4. The Fugitive
5. There is this new movie called Ekò… Its a Malayalam Movie, give it a try.
6. The Invisible Man 2020

Rebecca 1940
7. prisoners is a good one if you haven't seen it, keeps you hooked the whole time
8. The forgotten 
9. Fall
10. The woman in the window

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which moment or scene from a Tarantino movie is your favorite and has stayed memorable for a long time?

**Score:** 1695
**Body:** For me, this scene from Kill Bill: Volume 2 has remained quite memorable to this day.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bill:
You hocked a Hattori Hanzon Sword?

Budd:
Yep.

Bill:
It was priceless.

Budd:
Well, not in El Paso, it ain't. In El Paso I got me $250 for it.

edit: RIP to Michael Madsen. My favorite recurring actor in Tarantino movies. Loved the energy he brought on screen.
  - >!Which makes the golf bag reveal so much more awesome!!<
    - "To my brother Budd, the only man I ever loved - Bill"
2. ![gif](giphy|Y5WErzlBGtxDO)
  - I also like the line where he's called a sore loser so he says, "and you are an abysmal winner." I think of that distinction pretty often.
3. “If this is it old boy, I hope you don’t mind I go out speaking the King’s”
4. The “what now” dialog between Butch and Marcellus Wallace in PF is gold 
  - "You ok?"

"Nah, man. I'm pretty fucking far from ok"
5. The battle with O-Ren Ishii is one of my all-time favorite movie scenes. The soundtrack, background, action, and cinematography were just perfect. 
6. Inglorious Basterds intro scene. It was so intense and the dialogue is exceptional. 

## 2. Which actor/actress has the best nerd street cred based on their body of work? Round 1 - Part 1: Ron Perlman vs Karl Urban

**Score:** 768
**Body:** **Ron Perlman**

Sci-Fi franchise: ✅ Alien: Resurrection ✅ Pacific Rim

Comic book adaptation: ✅ Hellboy ✅ Blade 2

Fantasy franchise: ✅ Season of the Witch ✅ Conan the Barbarian

Literary classic adaptation: ✅ Beauty and the Beast ✅ The Name of the Rose

Video game adaptation: ✅ Monster Hunter

————————————————————

**Karl Urban**

Sci-Fi franchise: ✅ Star Trek ✅ Chronicles of Riddick

Comic book adaptation: ✅ The Boys ✅ Dredd ✅ Thor: Ragnarok

Fantasy franchise: ✅ The Lord of the Rings ✅ Pathfinder

Spy thriller: ✅ The Bourne Supremacy

Video game adaptation: ✅ Doom ✅ Mortal Kombat 2

**Comment your vote below**

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Typically tournaments don’t start with top seeded teams playing each other in round one.
  - If you have a contender for third place I would discuss with you. Alan Tudyk is working his way up, but idk who else is in this discussion. Idris Elba has a good resume but still leagues in the rear view mirror 
    - Ian McKellen has the LOTR and X-Men franchises, The Golden Compass, Stardust, a Stephen King adaptation (Apt Pupil) and Sherlock Holmes. Not quite as deep as Perlman and Urban, but he has two characters more iconic than anyone either of those two have played.

Harrison Ford has Han Solo, Indiana Jones, Rick Deckard, Jack Ryan, General Ross, Cowboys & Aliens.
    - Andy Serkis just hanging back like the shark in Jaws
2. Mortal Kombat, Trek, Marvel and LOTR...dude just needs a Star wars role and he would be entering Christopher Lee territory 
  - What if I told you he was in a Star Wars movie?
3. Ron Perlman is also the iconic John Fallout from the intro sequences
  - And now Perlman is playing a Super Mutant in the Fallout Show
4. Perlman gets even more credit for having been a voice actor on a bunch of videogames, and the original narrator of Fallout
5. Karl Urban. Very tough choice.

## 3. What should be added to this list?

**Score:** 140

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Matrix lobby scene. 
  - This one probably takes it as the best action scene in the film, but the Morpheus vs Neo training scene and the Neo vs Agent Smith scene in the Subway are also iconic.  
  - The fact this is absent from the list, as well as any of the Bourne movies, makes me think the criteria is aimed at a different audience.  
2. Ip Man.  Donny Yen vs 10
3. Omaha Beach, Saving Private Ryan 
4. Eastern Promises sauna/bathhaus scene. Probably the most realistic and brutal of all of these… and you get Viggo dong! 
5. HEAT not being on this list is criminal. 
6. The film Bullitt: The car chase scene, iconic
7. Termination 2 truck chase scene 
8. Any scene in Mad Max Fury Road 
(It doesn’t specify fights, just action scenes)

## 4. Film and performance that made you a fan of Robert Pattinson

**Score:** 105
**Body:** Performance: Remember Me

Film: Good Time

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lighthouse 
2. Good time 
  - The movie is just so good.  It’s a great script and filmed well but his acting is top notch.  He credibly and realistically pulls off a character who continually makes the worst choices repeatedly, the entire movie.  I see so many fuckups I’ve known in my own personal life embodied in his performance, and the plot is all just so crazy but I’ve had some crazy nights like that myself and none of it stretches the imagination.  

Pattinson has a Brad-Pitt-like presence and ability, probably better.  I think he will likely be with us for a long time.
3. The Lighthouse

*Processing img j1i0qi0z6upg1...*
  - The kid from Twilight totally holding his own against an absolutely unhinged Willem Dafoe for an entire movie?

That's what did it for me. Though he was actually pretty good in Goblet of Fire too. 
4. The Devil All Of The Time
5. Tenet. 
6. The Rover.
7. The Lighthouse which then led me to Good Time. 

He’s currently one of my favorite working actors. 
8. Honestly I was a fan since he was Cedric Diggory in Harry Potter: The Goblet of Fire

## 5. Peter Jackson's King Kong (2005)

**Score:** 92
**Body:** I seriously do not understand the hate this movie gets. This film is pure adventure from as soon as they arrive at Skull Island to very end with the tragic passing of Kong. The fight scenes are well done and put with the giant bugs is absolutely terrifying. The CGI holds up pretty well and I really enjoyed Jack Black and Kyle Chandler's characters. Overall, its a fantastic 'popcorn movie' in which you watch this to have a great time and I didn't know that 3 hours had passed when the credits finally rolled. My rating  is 4 out of 5 stars.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A worthy remake, for once!!!

![gif](giphy|PUVEcnBULWs0oAE0f0|downsized)
2. I love it after the first hour 
  - This was like 3 movies in one. 
3. One of my favourite movies of all time which is probably not a very popular opinion. It's definitely not perfect, but I loved every minute of it unapologetically. 
4. I really liked it too. I watched it not expecting anything other than a good time. Were people expecting morality lessons or something? 
5. I showed clips of this movie to my kids before we visited the Empire State Building a couple years ago. I won’t spoil it but for anyone that has ever visited the Empire State Building, but the trip to the top leans very heavily into King Kong lore! Really have the kids full on believing that a giant ape once got loose from the zoo and climbed the tall buildings in NYC. For them, this movie helps put King Kong up there with Santa Claus. They’ll get it one day, when they grow up, but it’s all very real for time being and anything is possible.
6. I love this movie. 
7. I enjoyed this movie a lot, and still do. I thought it was pure spectacle and it's been a while since I've seen a popcorn movie as enjoyable to watch in a theater.

Also the game adaptation of this film is fantastic...which, for a licensed game based on a movie not named Goldeneye, is an incredible achievement. 
8. Recent rewatch and enjoyed it immensley.  The dinosaur sequences were great, hard to believe they came out so good way back then.  All the sequences with Kong were great. 
9. Yeah, really enjoyed it! Loved the 30s vibe, and the cast was just perfect, imo.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Why do modern films look so visually bad?

**Score:** 41
**Body:** This is a problem I’ve had with a lot of films for a few years now, but after watching today’s Spider Man trailer I feel like something is seriously off with the way modern movies are shot and lit. Why does a huge, expensive blockbuster like Spider Man sometimes look like it was filmed in someone’s basement?

I genuinely love movies so it’s frustrating seeing so many of them end up with this grey, washed out, almost sludgy look. The composition is always so awful. What bothers me even more is how many people seem to think it actually looks good.

I’m not trying to start an argument here, I’m honestly just curious. Why do modern movies look like this? Did.  cinematographers and lighting crews just forget how to do their jobs or is something else going on?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hugely expensive movies made by committe have no visual identity and their lack of planning and vision means everything comes out a gray wash. They can't decide on weapons or costumes so it all has to be done in post. Then it becomes a game of having to hide so much CGI, leading to the dim look of a lot of these. The vfx teams are victims of no planning or vision as well, they're forced to churn out version after version until they land on something before some executive changes their minds again. 

Then look at Dune Part 2. An expensive movie that looks expensive, helmed by a director with vision and the capability to plan and execute said vision because he knows what he wants.
  - Movie studios are essentially “Private Equitying” themselves nowadays. They don’t really care about blowing away audiences as much as maintaining the bottom line and keeping stakeholders happy.

They think Becuase we have 3D, green screen and cheaper equipment we can do anything we want for less money.

Back in the day even cheesy movies like Transformers actually flew their cast and crew out to the real locations of the Great Pyramids, Scotland, shot on the streets of Thailand and truly made audiences feel as if they were being whisked away to offered parts of the world.

But the truth is I don’t believe audiences have actually ever accepted heavy CG as acceptable. And I think we are seeing more and more of audiences realizing when their brains see it, it really doesn’t register like practical effects once did.

My prediction has been that soon we are going to see a huge return to practical effects.

2. >Why does a huge, expensive blockbuster like Spider Man sometimes look like it was filmed in someone’s basement?

It doesn't. It looks like it's a huge, expensive blockbuster that has had all of its edges filed off by going through seven million levels of approval and does a lot of mid shit to get okay looking CGI at the cheapest price point possible that is still ridiculously expensive.
3. Did you watch any of the Best Picture nominees from last year? Many of them are shot gorgeously. Good movies are definitely still being made, but you have to get out of the Marvel and blockbuster bubble. Check out Bugonia - it has Kubrick vibes. Check out Hamnet, Sinners, Train Dreams, F1, and Frankenstein
4. This might not be what you're honing in on but a lot of cinematography is very bokeh-inspired shallow focus to get a pin sharp foreground (an actor, typically) and a kind blurred out light effect in the background. It looks like absolute ass and is used in nearly everything new. Deep focus is the old man yelling at clouds, except for occasional set pieces.
  - I get that this shallow focus craze is done to facilitate easier post-processing...but man, classic deep focus films by people like Kurosawa and Welles look SO DAMN GOOD. I just rewatched High and Low recently and I swear it's one of the most good looking movies ever made.
  - It’s crazy because some beautiful movies used similar shallow focus to emulate, say, old photographs like Deakins did on film in Assassination of Jesse James, or at least what they tried to do on Arri Alexas in Train Dreams. 

But it has to be for a purpose and you have to do it right. In those cases it’s pretty to look at but it’s also deliberately evocative of the time they’re portraying. Just going shallow focus (some argue to make more room to do fuck shit digitally in the background) isn’t evoking anything except portrait mode on your iPhone. 
5. The digitial sludge has been an issue that has been discussesd and debated for years. There isn't one reason, but several as to why this particular look has been prevalent.  
  
In some cases it's the nature of digital recording which smooths out the image and gives more latitude to tweak the image. Then we have an explosion of different types of screens to watch films on which all have their different set of color standards. Next we have an industry that often copies the latest trend, and this is the current trend. Muted browns and greys are viewed as more realistic, which is what audiences clamor for. Younger generations want the "vibe". 

These are all professionals so it has nothing to do with competence. This is a choice that is being made during pre-production. By the way, when you mention composition that has nothing to do with color.

You can see subtle changes watching films from specific decades to see what were the latest trends and stylistic options cinematographers were choosing from. Hopefully this current trend will end soon. Not all films choose the digital sludge style as evident by a couple of best picture nominees this year.
6. CGI

It's easier to use heavy amounts of unplanned CGI when the focus and lighting is largely uniform. Disney isn't storyboarding every individual scene with the VFX teams on site, they will do all the principal photography with a rough idea of what they want to see of big CGI set pieces, and then fill in later. Camera lenses and lighting is chosen to maximize the generic look, this gives VFX teams the easiest time turning out passable CG assets at the lowest cost possible.

There are still great directors/studios who use CGI properly. The recent Disney releases, not really.
7. Here’s one pretty good breakdown of some reasons why: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=tvwPKBXEOKE

But it’s kind of become a genre of article and video now so there are plenty of other discussions about it online. There are a variety of factors contributing to this, and different people have tackled different ones. 

## 2. Sentimental Value

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I know I’ve seen previous threads on here hoping to re-ignite some discourse in this film. Phenomenal movie. There’s only slight logic things that confuse me that I hope others can offer new perspectives on. 

Primarily talking about the ending here. I remember before seeing it (as I saw it only a week ago actually) people would say oh the ending FLOORED me, which had me excited to watch I enjoy a good cry and emotional blanket on a movie. So, Ok I understand the weight of Nora reading the script for the movie and having this realization, but what realization is that? If the idea is that she is visibly moved/emotional reading it because she now sees that her father understands her human condition and “sees her” and she then does the movie, carries out that scene and we get this unspoken communication of emotions understanding between the two characters of father-daughter for once in their lives, fine, that makes plenty of sense. My thing here is that it is implied throughout the movie that Gustav has been significantly and largely absent from his daughter’s lives. So given that, how the hell could he so accurately know what she’s going through that he can express his understanding and sorrow for it by way of a film script. Now I doubt he’s so absent as to mean that their meeting at the what seems to be a post funeral/post wake thing at the house towards the beginning of the film is the first time in a long time as he at least confirms he’s seen Agnes’ son at least once before, noting how he’s grown. It’s implied that they’ve seen each other, but more of a here and there basis, nothing frequent, nothing consistent. So with that logic, is the audience to assume/accept that Nora’s character is just that potent and evident to Gustav in their few and far between meetings that he just knows exactly what she’s going through as a person and can relate to her through this script. 

Interested to hear other people interpretations. This part is just what confused me ab the film and the importance of the script that links their two traumas together. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My interpretation was that, whilst he didn't know the exact details of what Nora was going through, he recognised in her things he saw in his mother and likely also himself. It was less a factual understanding of her situation, and more an instinctive emotional one. 

One of the key themes of the film is how trauma is passed down through generations. The obvious parallel is between Nora and his mother, via their suicide attempts. However, the film shows us repeatedly how similar Nora and her father actually are, despite their communication issues. 

There are the obvious similarities: they both work in the arts and process their pain through their art; they both struggle to maintain romantic attachments; they both struggle to communicate how they really feel. Additionally, during the birthday party scenes, there's lot of work done to show how they mirror each other. For example, when Nora makes a joke about her brother-in-law, Gustav picks up on it immediately and chuckles whereas she needs to repeat herself for the other attendees to understand. When they're outside smoking, and for once aren't trying to communicate verbally, it's one of the closest moments between them in the film. The same goes for the ending scene where they communicate non-verbally.

Gustav experienced enormous trauma at a young age and seemingly coped with it through womanising, alcohol, and processing his pain through his art. When you've been through the kind of thing he's been through, it makes it easier to recognise that pain in another person. It doesn't really matter than he doesn't know the ins and outs of her life. He can sense it, because he's so familiar with it. He wants to reach out to her and let her know that he sees that pain in her and knows how it feels, but he always says the wrong thing, so the script was his way of doing so. 
2. Gustav, like a lot of men, and like whole generations of male artists, understands his life and communicates about it primarily _through_ his work. 

It might help for you to think of certain obvious models that may have informed his character — directors like Lars von Trier (a friend of Skarsgård’s) and Ingmar Bergman. Both of these Scandinavian auteurs made widely acclaimed films about the human condition while also having messy personal lives, and even distant cold relationships with their own children.

That’s how he can see his daughter and use her for material while spinning it into an amalgam of fact and fiction that’s so deeply true about some of her innermost feelings. It’s what he’s always done. It’s how he works.
  - These are excellent observations and I feel much the same as you describe. 
I don’t think it’s just a lot of men or male artists, but specifically that as a successful renowned director, he would be a student of the human condition and emotions.  So I think that he would’ve been able to feel his daughters' resentments or, in the younger daughter’s case, the wish for family reunification.  I felt that the younger daughter’s role was extremely pivotal, and I loved her character deeply.
3. I see the script that Gustav creates as his metaphorical apology or explanation for why he is the way he is (a absent, emotionally avoidant, asshole father). His childhood, where his mom is tortured and then kills herself makes Gustav the person that he is today. In the same way, Nora is who she is because of her relationship (or lack thereof) with her father. So, Gustav and Nora are both connecting (for the first time in their lives) through this shared trauma. They both went through it. When the daughters read the script, they finally understand their father. This is especially true for Nora, because in Gustav's script he is describing how his mother kills herself (In the same way Nora does coincidentally), She sees the trauma her grandmother went through, which gave trauma to her father, which gave her trauma. The final scene where the suicide is acted out is the culmination of this connection between father and daughter.

The script isn't about Nora, it's about why Gustav is the way he is, and it is for his daughters to see. It's his final, desperate attempt to connect with his daughters in the only way he knows how, through his art. Nora is the lead in his film to show that the generational trauma from the grandmother has made its way all the way to her in a cyclical nature. 
4. I don't think Gustav knows the details of what his daughter has gone through, but he implicitly understands the emotions she carries as he and his mother have carried the same emotional weight. The script didn't describe Nora's life but his mothers. 

To me at least.
  - This. I feel that Gustav found an outlet in filmmaking, whereas his mother didn’t and we saw how that ended. By writing a script centered on his daughter, he’s not only exploring the family’s generational trauma, he’s encouraging his daughter to have the same outlet and trying to help in the only way he knows how.  

The ending has an interesting parallel with Hamnet if you think of Gustav as Shakespeare. 

5. I don’t think that he knew what happened to her. I think it was more that she reminded him of his mother (and also himself). The daughters say that they had never known this happened to their father right? I think, for Nora, finally understanding what he had been through made her open to building a connection with him again. 

I can relate a lot as someone who had an incredibly emotionally distant parent growing up. Mine was also the same in that they experienced an incredible amount of childhood trauma that they are reluctant to open up about. I still don’t think I know fully everything that happened to them, but sometimes just hearing a little more helps you forgive them for having such a dysfunctional communication style.  

I think an interpretation of the ending as “the two are fine and healed all their issues” is a massive oversimplification (not that you are saying that but I have seen take a lot as a criticism of the ending). We see at the ending that neither Nora or her father are able to communicate their feelings to each other.  But the door is open and that is the real hope of the ending. 
6. I'm just going to quote [my comment from three months ago](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1pemv32/comment/nse9acp/):

>I think the film is constantly hinting that not everything is as it seems with Gustav.

>We know that Gustav is the son of a woman who suffered a lot of trauma, and ended up killing herself. So he was absent a parent growing up. Similarly, Nora was absent a parent growing up. You know the consequences for Nora in particular have been pretty bad, culminating in her suicide attempt. We know about Gustav that the only times he ever calls Nora is when he's drunk. We also see him get drunk in the film, and indeed, he ends up calling Nora. He gets so drunk towards the end of the film that he ends up in the hospital, nearly killing himself.

>And here's the clincher: Nora reads the screenplay, and asks her sister if she had told Gustav about Nora's suicide attempt. She had not. So how did Gustav know?

>There's a very telling shot in the last third of the film, a surreal shot that shows Gustav's face overlapping the two daughters, in a way that is not dissimilar from the famous shot in Persona. It seems to have the implication that Gustav and his two children are more like than they think.

>So again, how did Gustav know what it was like to be in a suicidal mindset? The answer, as I see it, is that Gustav himself suffers the same trauma, as inherited from his mother, as passed to his daughter. So what Nora saw in that screenplay was not just herself, it was her father. He's also been suicidal. And so Nora sees herself in Gustav, and Gustav sees himself in Nora.

>It's because of that recognition, I think, that Nora agrees to do the movie.

## 3. Les Rayons Et Les Ombres Starring Jean Dujardin Film Review.

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Les Rayons et les Ombres is french movie directed by Xavier Giannoli, this movie is a powerful historical drama exploring the true story of journalist Jean Luchaire and his daughter Corinne during France's Occupation in World War II. Starting as pacifists promoting Franco-German friendship in the 1920s, Jean (played by Jean Dujardin) and his friend Otto Abetz descend into collaboration, Jean becomes a press magnate advocating for the occupiers, while Corinne rises as a young movie star.

The film masterfully depicts ambition, compromise, and moral decay, framed by Corinne's 1948 reflections, culminating in tragedy.

Jean Dujardin's astonishing performance stands out nuanced, intense, and magnetic, he captures the character's tragic contradictions, charismatic yet compromised, this is one of his career-best performances, far from his lighter roles.

This movie can be Compared to French cinema war occupation classics movies like Lacombe Lucien (moral ambiguity under Occupation) or Au revoir les enfants (youth and war), it echoes their depth but stands out with epic scope.

The movie cinematography is outstanding it,s a 70 millions dollars movie amazing meticulous period recreation, and unflinching gaze on french collaboration during the war, it,s potentially one of the most important French films of the last decades.

Jean Dujardin's is famous for the Oscar-winning masterpiece movie The Artist (2011).

Les Rayons et les Ombres is a masterful and epic on moral compromise and the shadows of collaboration-elevated by Jean Dujardin's astonishing performance.

This movie resonates with today's global politics, the slippery slope from idealism and "dialogue" to moral compromise, media complicity with extremism, populism's rise, and justifications for aligning with authoritarian forces echo contemporary debates with far-right ideologies and ethical drifts in power and influence worldwide.

This movie is a masterpiece a great big screen experience, it,s gripping and immersive with historical resonance, and powerful questions about humanity.

A highly recommended movie.

## 5. Why is it that whenever we hear or read about stories that executive producers and directors/writers clash with each other, the executive producers tend to be wrong about how to make films? Shouldn't executive producers know what they are doing?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I guess that this is the reality in every single medium, whether it is the actual manager on a production company or a random person in a suit.

I tend to encounter stories that if a film does not go well or as planned, then it is mostly because the executive producers thought the wrong decisions on what is best for the films.

If executive producers tend to make the wrong decisions, then how come this is a common thing?

Is there a pattern or some kind of logic on how producers think versus how creators think?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hmmm. When an executive producer *does* override a director and it actually improves a film, I don't think we hear about it. All glory goes to the director. We're more likely to hear about it when a producer meddles against the director's wishes and the result is bad! 
  - >All glory goes to the director. 

In fairness the best picture Oscar is awarded to the producer not the director and there's a reason for that. The producer oversee's the entire project. The producer chooses the director and matches them to the script. Ultimately the producer has final say over everything, which is why directors will often leave a project if they're butting heads over the creative direction. You rarely hear about the *producer* leaving a project due to creative differences...

This tends to get overlooked because Oscar winning directors are often 'auteur' directors who write and produce the films they direct.
  - It's TV, but Tony Gilroy's original plan for Andor S1 was for Marva's speech to end with "Fuck the Empire" but the studio made him change it to "Fight the Empire," which I think works far better. Gilroy actually kept fighting the studio about it. 

Fight the Empire is a call to action, so it works in a narrative sense, and the entire scene would be subsumed by online takes about Star Wars first F-bomb if they left it in. It would overwhelm the rest of the speech and detract from the message. 
  - Cinema Paradiso is an example of the producer over riding the director and getting a master piece in the process. I just watched the director's cut and it takes away all the mystery, magic and charm. It was Harvey Weinstein who wanted the cuts, so of course everyone had to hear about it.
2. It definitely happens all the time. Just look at all the director's cuts of movies that are worse than theatrical (excluding the ones forced to be made by the producers).

But the producers aren't the ones telling the stories, and the filmmakers aren't telling stories about times that producers showed them up and proved them wrong.
  - It's the man bites dog phenomenon where we remember the unusual cases.
3. An executive producer is essentially an investor, or someone who convinced investors to fund a film. Their primary motivation is making sure that the film is as profitable as possible. 

A director or a writer's primary motivation is usually making the film as they want it to be. They have an artistic motivation, or at least a vision of what the film should be like in their heads. 

These two motivations can sometimes be at cross-purposes with each other. 

> If executive producers tend to make the wrong decisions

Here's the thing - they don't. You are only hearing about famous cases where they did. If an executive was constantly making the wrong decisions, they wouldn't have their job for very long.
  - >An executive producer is essentially an investor, or someone who convinced investors to fund a film. 

Not all EPs are. Studio and production company heads, people involved in development, as well as other roles all garner executive producer credits.
4. Good producers save movies all the time, you just don’t hear about it. When a movie is good, very few directors would be willing to say on record, “thank god my producers talked me out of that dumb idea I had”. When a movie is bad, everyone points fingers, creatives at execs and execs at creatives. People just latch onto the studio meddling version more because good artist against bad bureaucrat is a more appealing story.
5. From the storiers I remember, some producers tend to think more about marketability and profit while the cast and director think of the artistic output.  Ex., a producer/studio may push a rising star a director doesn't think is right for the part.

A recent example would be Ben Affleck talking about how execs at Neflix want plot points repeated to accomodate people scrolling on their phones, which is not something a writer generally considers.

This doesn't mean the producer is always wrong.  I remember one movie where the studio or producers opted for a more optimistic ending, and I thought it was the better choice (The Butterfly Effect?).
